If you are new here, my wife & I are on the #eatjerseychallenge to visit and eat a meal in every town in New Jersey. There are officially 564 towns in NJ, each with a story to tell. Follow along for food pics and town trivia as we make our way to every corner of the Garden State.

I’ve been a map geek since I was a kid. My wife and I are foodies and love to eat out. Most of our meals are “blessed” by my wife with (the ghost of Steve) Jobs with a photo before they can be eaten. All of those photos just sit on our camera rolls, eating up space, with no one ever seeing them.

Until… we got this great idea from our friends Chris & Jessica. They started a challenge to visit and eat a meal in every town in Massachusetts (all 351 of them!).

This map geek & foodie thought… We’re a small state. We can do this in JERSEY! 

There are officially 564 towns in Jersey. And each one has a story to tell.

Most folks know the big foodie towns in Jersey…. Asbury Park, Hoboken, Red Bank, Montclair, etc…. Of course we’ve been to most of those before.

But have you ever eaten in Elsinboro?  Or Alpha?! Yeah, Alpha… that’s a town!  Well, we have!

It turns out, over the past 10 years, my wife and I had eaten in 87 of Jersey’s towns.  We started counting towns with #88 in July 2023.

Since then, we’ve been sharing food pics, town trivia, and more from each town with our friends & family.  We’re almost a 3rd of the way there. Now, we’re inviting the rest of the world to follow us on our food journey to every corner of the great Garden State.

Follow us here on the socials @eatjerseychallenge.  Hopefully we’ll see you in YOUR Jersey town soon!

New Jersey Town 129: Lawrence, Mercer County.

We hit the Route 1 Diner for Sunday morning breakfast. Just over the line from the State Capitol, we HAD to try the Trenton Omelet, loaded with Pork Roll. We also grabbed the Chicken Avocado Omelet. Both were solid options at this 
Classic Jersey diner.

Now here's what got me. The diner's got Albert Einstein on the wall. But Einstein never lived in Lawrence, he lived in Princeton, the next town over. But THIS guy (@jonstewart)… he grew up HERE!

Established  in 1697, Lawrence was originally called Maidenhead. In 1816, the Presbyterian church decided that sounded a little too much like "virginity", and they couldn't handle it. So they renamed it after Captain James Lawrence, a War of 1812 naval hero famous for his dying command: "Don't give up the ship!"

The town's historic center is called "Lawrenceville". With historic homes and buildings, as well as the prestigious Lawrenceville Boarding School.  Lawrenceville has its own post office that serves the majority of the town. 

Because of this, most people refer to the entire town as "Lawrenceville"… but it's not.  It's just Lawrence.  And THAT is something we learned on this stop as I always thought it was Lawrenceville too!

Next time you're in the area, check out the Route 1 Diner or one of the other small businesses in town.

New Jersey Town 128: Hopatcong

We stopped by the Hopatcong Diner for the best meal of the day: Dessert! In fact, it was our first dessert stop since we started the challenge.

After dinner in nearby Sparta, we made a dessert run to the Hopatcong Diner. Our FIRST dessert stop since we started the challenge. Dessert is a meal, so we're counting it!

From the outside it looks more like a dive bar than a diner, but inside? Total Jersey diner vibes!

We had their Apple Pie à la Mode, and the Flan, along with some tea and coffee — the perfect ending to a night out up in Sussex County. 

Hopatcong sits on the western shore of Lake Hopatcong, and it’s one of four towns wrapped around the largest freshwater lake in New Jersey.

The area was once known as "Brookland".

In the late 1800s, the railroad arrived and the lake exploded into a tourist mecca, with resorts and development spreading around to the west side of the lake, which had previously been remote.

In 1898, the residents of the growing Brookland community voted to form their own town. 

They named it Brooklyn, mostly to sound familiar to all the New Yorker's that made it a summer resort.

But that got confusing fast. Mail for Brooklyn, NJ was ending up in Brooklyn, NY! 

So three years later, they renamed the town Hopatcong, after the lake all those New Yorkers were coming to see.

One of the lake’s most fascinating residents was Hudson Maxim. An inventor and chemist, who made his fortune by creating and selling explosives which were later used in WW1. He built a boathouse on the lake that looked like a medieval castle because…. Why not?! 

He also donated the land that became Modick Park, giving the town one of its central public spaces.

Next time you're in Hopatcong, be sure to check out the Hopatcong Diner or one of the other small businesses in town!

New Jersey Town 127: Sparta

We stopped by Il Porto on Lake Mohawk. We had the scallops with creamed eggplant special, and this was the absolute star of the show. We also had their Chicken Milanese and the Grilled Salmon.

Sparta is known as the "Gateway to Sussex county", and you feel it the second the Route 15 freeway ends and you're suddenly on quiet, single-lane country roads. 

At the center of it all is Lake Mohawk, with its boardwalk and Alpine‑style town center. Featuring Tudor buildings, charming shops, and restaurants, Sparta is one of those places that feels like a mini-escape from the rest of Jersey.
The lake itself was built in 1926 by a development company, and today about 2,600 families live around it on private land.
Sparta’s history isn’t just lakes and leisure. This area was full of iron, zinc, and limestone mines. One of those mine owners? Thomas Edison, Jersey's famous light bulb inventor. One of the mines he started still operates today under the Braen Stone Company!
We're sad to report, that after 15 years in business, il Porto has closed for good. 

Next time you're Sparta, be sure to check out the new Angeloni's in its place or one of the many other small businesses in town.

PS: We saw that Angeloni's OG location in Caldwell got an 8.8 from Portnoy!

New Jersey Town 126: South River

We stopped by the Portuguese Fisherman. Serving up Iberian cuisine since 1976, this community staple is one of the oldest Portuguese restaurants in the US.

We started with the Polvo Grelhado, beautifully grilled octopus.
Then the Bitoque: Steak served Portuguese style with fried egg and ham.
We shared their Paella Valencia, and wrapped it up with the Crème Catalana, Spain's silky cousin to crème brulee.

We loved this spot. Every dish delivered, service was great, and it was the perfect place to share a meal with our family!

Settled by Europeans in 1720, the town became known as Washington. In 1898, when the community broke away from East Brunswick, the state said, "Ummm… we already have SEVEN Washingtons in Jersey. Could you please pick a different name?!"

So they named the town after the SOUTH river, which ironically flows NORTH, along the towns EASTERN border!

Toxic waste? Yeah, Jersey's got a rep for burying things, but brace yourself for this one…
Hitler's hand-picked leader for the puppet government of Belarus during the Nazi Occupation of WW2, is buried HERE in South River!
WAIT… WHAT?!
After the war, Radoslaw Ostrowski allegedly bounced from Germany to Argentina and finally Benton Harbor, Michigan where he spent his final years. 
How he ended up buried here?
Maybe one for our friends at Ghosts on the Coast to look into!

Stop everything! Brunch just dropped at Aby's Mexican in Matawan (@abysmexicanrestaurant), and it's WAY too good not to share!

When we're not traveling the state, Aby's has always been one of our hometown favorites.  Their Pollo Mole Poblano and Camarones al Ajo are our "go-to" dinner orders!

So when we heard they launched a brand-new weekend brunch menu, we had to check it out!

We kicked things off with the Chorizo Benedict. This dish doesn’t just show up, it shows OFF!
Buttery brioche toast stacked with smoky Mexican chorizo, baby spinach, creamy avocado, and perfectly poached eggs under a jalapeño cream sauce. 
Next up, the Chorizo Poblano Omelette, it’s that perfect mix of smoky, cheesy, and peppery… the kind of omelette that wakes you up fast!
For the sweet side, we had to try their Croffles. Crisp, buttery croissant layers pressed into a waffle, topped with fresh berries, tres leches sauce, and vanilla whipped cream.
And finally, the Lechera Pancakes were cloud‑soft, and dripping with caramel sauce. Basically dessert masquerading as breakfast.  Trust me, we’re not complaining.
Next time you're in Matawan, if you already love Aby’s for dinner, their brunch is your next obsession. Weekends only! Go hungry and enjoy every bite.

New Jersey Town #125: Pine Beach

We just stepped out of the Sand Castle Diner in Beachwood when—wait… is that a Roy Rogers across the street?! I haven’t seen a Roys since the ‘90s! 

It turns out, Route 9 is the town line—and that Roy’s? It’s in another town. So, on to our next stop!We’ve been avoiding chains on this tour, but this was a nostalgic exception.

At Roy’s, your burger comes naked. Then it’s DIY at the legendary Fixin’s Bar.

Once 600+ strong, now just 40 Roys remain. Started by Marriott Hotels in the ‘60s and named after the famous actor, it was bought by Hardee’s in the ‘90s, then nearly vanished! Now they’re making a comeback, with 3 in Jersey and counting.

Established in 1925, Pine Beach got its start In 1908, when a Philly businessman dropped $10K on riverfront land along the new rail line in Berkeley Township.

His salesman pitched homes from the train, as it crossed over the Toms River, showing off the shoreline. By year’s end: 109 deeds sold, 22 homes built.

The Berkeley locals didn't give much attention to the mostly seasonal residents of Pine Beach. Unpaved streets, garbage buried in yards. So in 1925, in a Jersey tale that's as old as time, Pine Beach voted to go solo and become their own town so they could control where their taxes went.

Next time you're in Pine Beach, be sure to check out Roy Rogers, or one of the other small businesses in town.

New Jersey Town #124: Beachwood - A town dreamt up by a newspaper!

We kicked off our morning at the Sandcastle Diner, a Jersey Shore classic, with our first #EatJerseyChallenge guest, my aunt.

It was… Three Florentine Omelets, Two Italians, one Filipina, and one very full table! 

Located on the banks of the Toms River, Beachwood's story? It all starts with a newspaper!

In 1913, the New York Tribune bought land in Berkeley Township to build a summer resort—and boost subscriptions. The pitch? Buy a 20x100 foot plot for $19.60, and get six months of the paper for free. That’s about $620 in today's money.

By 1915, the first residents moved in. Plans rolled out fast: a clubhouse, pier, library, a community center—even a train station. With sailing and swimming—this place was built for summer dreams.

Two years later, residents voted to break away from Berkeley, and in 1917 Beachwood officially became its own town.

Today, houses in town average $450,000. Not a bad return for a newspaper and a summer dream!

Next time you're in Beachwood, be sure to stop by the Sand Castle Diner or one of the other small businesses in town.

NJ Town #123: Blairstown, where horror history meets small town charm.
We rolled into town on Friday the 13th in October to eat at the iconic Blairstown Diner. But so did every Gen X'er with a hockey mask and a selfie-stick!

So we had to settle for this picture.

That diner? It's where the 1980 horror classic, Friday the 13th, filmed its opening scenes.
The rest of the movie? Shot at a Boy Scout camp in the next town over.

So we pivoted, thanks to a local tip, and landed at the Buck Hill Brewery & Restaurant just outside downtown. No masked killers in sight. Just killer food! 

We had the mussels with white sauce, the seared rare tuna, provolone stuffed meatballs, a South of the border salad with steak, and wrapped it up with an apple blossom that deserves its own sequel.

Established in 1845, Blairstown was named after it's most famous resident… NO, not Jason Voorhees, but rather John Insley Blair, a local boy who became a "train daddy" during the gilded age, owning and running multiple railroads across the country right here from his cozy hometown lair.

Main Street's a year-round draw, with charming shops, galleries, and restaurants. In October, it's cosplay central. But any time's good for ice cream, Blair Lake strolls, or a walk through Footbridge Park.

Next time you're in Blairstown, be sure to check out the Buck Hill Brewery & Restaurant, or one of the many small businesses in town!

NJ Town #122: Clinton (the Town, not Township) 

Clinton is one of those cute destination towns, full of hidden gems.

Except… it's not really hidden! 
I don't know how they did it, but this place got itself advertised on highway signs as far away as Newark Airport!

Even if you've never been to Clinton, you've probably seen it's famous Red Mill. It's in almost every Jersey tourism ad and magazine about the state. 

That famous Red Mill goes back to 1810, when it was originally used for wool processing.
Every October, the mill is transformed into a haunted house called the Red Mill Haunted Village.

We stopped by The River Mill View, named for its outdoor patio views of… the Red Mill!

We shared their Beet Salad as starter. For entrees, we went with their Black Angus Burger, and the grilled Octopus

Time for town trivia: There are 5 types of municipalities in Jersey.

Established in 1865, Clinton is neither a village, nor a boro, nor a city, nor a township. 
Clinton is one of only 15 actual TOWNS in the state!

Its historic downtown is packed with cozy restaurants and charming small businesses—perfect for a crisp Fall weekend escape.

If you're looking for a fun day out, be sure to visit Clinton and check out the River Mill View or one of the MANY small businesses in town.

NJ Town #121: Union Township (Hunterdon County)

You know you've arrived in Union when you see the Clinton Station Diner calling from the side of I-78! One of Jersey’s few remaining 24/7 diners—it’s a beacon for hungry travelers and roadside nostalgia.

We had the avocado toast with poached eggs,
The Rib-eye steak & eggs,
And to round it out, a short stack of French toast. 

And we had it served in a restored coach from Jersey's legendary Blue Comet train! The Blue Comet was a luxury train that ran from Liberty State Park in Jersey City to Atlantic City from 1929 to 1941. It was designed to provide passengers with deluxe accommodations and service at a regular coach fare.

Railfans have long been obsessed with the fabulous Blue Comet. Lionel released a model toy train set in the 1930s that goes for quite a sum of money on the collectors market.

Sopranos fans may recall how Bobby Baccalieri's obsession with the Blue Comet proved to be a fateful distraction! 

While the blue comet did not travel anywhere near Union, NJ, the Biela observation car, named for Biela's Comet, was restored and moved to Union by the diner's previous owners in the 1970s.

If you want to experience a piece of Jersey history, next time you're in Union, be sure to visit the Clinton Station Diner. So long from this whistle stop, follow along and we'll see you soon at our next stop!

New Jersey Town #120: Union (Union County)

We stopped by Peruvian Flavors. 

This was our first-ever Peruvian food experience, and wow!
We started strong with the Ceviche bathed in leche de tigre.
For drinks, we split a pitcher of Chicha Morada—a sweet purple corn drink. Trust us, it hits.
Then for entrees: Bistec a lo pobre. And Pollo Saltado. It was flavor overload!

After our recent trip to Peru, we can honestly say—this place brings the flavor home to Jersey!

Established in 1808, Union is recognized by many who pass the world's tallest water sphere on the side of the Garden State Parkway. (Usually while stuck in traffic). 

Union has the largest Home Depot in the world. And we're not talking about an extra aisle or two… at 217,000 square feet, the super store in the Vauxhall neighborhood is over DOUBLE the size of the average Home Depot!

As if that weren't big enough, Union has TWO Home Depots! Guess they're really serious about home improvement here?

Jersey is famous for its jughandles and no left turns allowed on most state highways.

But when you're in Union, they let you do it. You can do anything… Grab the wheel and just pull it left! You can do anything on this crazy stretch of Route 22, they let you take your life into your own hands.

Next time you're in Union, if you survive Route 22, be sure to check out Peruvian Flavors or one of the other small businesses in town!

We've been posting additional town trivia that didn't make the cut into each town's reel as stories. We took a poll, and folks said we should publish the bonus trivia as their own posts!

So here we go with the bonus town trivia for Highland Park!

NJ Town #119: Highland Park

We stopped by Mr. Pi's Sushi where we both got the sushi & sashimi combo. --fresh, colorful, and just what I needed after watching Rutgers obliterate my Virginia Tech Hokies at SHI Stadium.

Established in 1905, Highland Park is rich in history. Since 1921, it's proud World War 1 Doughboy statue has stood at the intersection of Raritan and Woodbridge Avenues in the middle of town honoring its heroes…

Until a truck leveled it in 2020! 

It got knocked down, but it got up again, rising in time for its 100th anniversary in 2021. Still standing. Still saluting.

Ever sliced a finger while making a sandwich? Thank Highland Park for your Band-Aid fix!
Back in 1921, Earle Dickson’s wife kept turning dinner into a first-aid situation—burns, cuts, nicks, the works. So Earle, a J&J employee, slapped a square of cotton on some surgical tape and boom—instant finger patch! His wife loved it. But even better, his bosses at J&J loved it and the Band-Aid was born. First-aid got cute, convenient, and kitchen-proof. 

Next time you're in Highland Park, be sure to check out Mr. Pi's Sushi or one of the other small businesses in town!

New Jersey Town #118: Dunellen

We stopped by Mis Amigos for Mexcian breakfast. We had the Huevos con Chorizo and the Carne Asada con Huevos. Both were excellent.

Established in 1887, this charming town grew around, and was named after, the Dunellen train station on the Raritan Valley Line.

But there's a mystery! No one knows how or where the station itself got its name! No grand founding myth, no revolution, just a place that grew because, apparently, people needed to get somewhere.

Known simply as "The Railroad Town", Dunellen is the only town in Middlesex County with a stop on the Raritan Valley Line.

People in Jersey love a great debate…
	• Pork Roll vs Taylor Ham
	• North Jersey vs South Jersey
	• Central Jersey vs one's imagination
	• And of course…. Wawa vs Quick Check

One of those debates started here on North Ave in 1967. That's when Quick Chek planted its flag and opened its first store. It was a humble beginning for what became a Jersey staple and a formidable challenger in the battle for hearts & minds for best convienence store in Jersey.

Next time you're in Dunellen, be sure to stop by Mis Amigos or one of the other small businesses in town.

NJ Town #117: Oceanport NOTE: We experienced The Nerds at Blu Grotto last season. They are not playing there in 2025.

 We stopped by the Beer Garden at The Blu Grotto, where the vibe is casual and relaxed, with live entertainment.  We were there to see The Nerds, one of Jersey's most beloved cover bands.

The summer time drinks were flowing with a rum bucket & a Kentucky Mule. For food, we mixed it up with some spring rolls, several Maki rolls, and a Neapolitan-style Margherita pizza that was really good, but somehow missed the camera lense.

Established in 1920, Oceanport is known for thoroughbred horse racing at Monmouth Park, a tradition since the 1800s. 

Until 2011, it was also the home of Fort Monmouth, an army base known for its communication tech. 
In the 1940s, Julius Rosenberg worked there and was convicted of spying for the Soviet Union, leading to his and his wife Ethel's execution—the only U.S. citizens ever executed for espionage.
After years of vacancy, Fort Monmouth is set to become a Netflix studio.
From Cold War secrets to Hollywood scripts, the fort's next act might just be a thrilling spy drama inspired by its own history!

Next time you're in Oceanport, be sure to check out the Blu Grotto, watch a race at the park, or catch the Nerds in concert!

New Jersey Town #116: Mount Laurel

We stopped by the Manila Café & Asian Mart.

With my wife being a Filipina, it was only a matter of time before we started hitting some Filipino places on the Eat Jersey Challenge.

This is “turo turo” style—Filipino for “point point.” No menus, no second-guessing. You walk up, you point, you eat. But we couldn’t say “no” to some Pork sisig: crispy, rich, unapologetic. Or the Pancit—noodles that carry the weight of tradition. And the real reason we came, the Halo Halo—a dessert that’s part chaos, part genius, and a great way to cool off on a warm summer day.

This is the hill. The one that gave the town its name. No grand entrance, no glossy brochures. Just a quiet patch of land, an un-marked New Jersey state park, where the laurel trees still stand thick. It’s the kind of place that doesn’t ask for attention, but rewards those who make the short climb to the top with a view of radio tower locked behind a chain link fence, forever beckoning area teenagers to overcome adversity and somehow find their way to the top for the real view of endless suburban paradise.

Established in 1872, Mount Laurel, is a name you’ve heard, maybe not for the reasons the town would like. A landmark in housing law, a battleground for fairness. But beyond the headlines, there’s a place worth knowing.

Next time you’re in Mount Laurel, be sure to visit the Manila Café or one of the other small businesses in town!

New Jersey Town #115: Hainesport
 
Okay, lifelong Jersey resident here, and I can honestly say, Hainesport was the first town we visited for the challenge that I’d NEVER heard before. We were here to see Big Rusty, a troll built by Danish sculptor, Thomas Dambo. Big Rusty was built out of recycled scrap from the abandoned Creek Turn Ceramic Company, on the banks of the Rancocas Creek.
 
Dambo has built over 150 trolls around the world, and you can find them all on his site’s troll map.
https://trollmap.com
 
Since 2023, Big Rusty has become so popular that the town bought the ceramic factory and plans to turn it into a park. It’s currently a graffiti alley, where spray artists show off their talents. If you’ve got small kids, watch out for debris, sharp edges, and images that depict 12-year old boy humor. And don’t try to feed or climb up on Big Rusty!
 
Speaking of food, we were hungry after seeing Big Rusty, so we stopped by @lillostomatopies. Portnoy ranked this place an 8.4, and we have to say, it was on point! Their classic Trenton-style tomato pie was excellent. It was a double meal for us, as we were told we had to try their Cheesesteaks too. Wow! The crisp and airy Semolina bread with a hint of butter made these steaks worth the trip. Heck, Portnoy gave the steaks a 9.1!
 
There might be a stereotype that South Jersey is all about cheesesteaks and pizza. This stop did not disavow that stereotype.
 
Next time you’re in Hainesport, be sure to visit Lillo’s and don’t miss Big Rusty!

NJ Town #114: Mount Holly

We stopped by B-Unos.

Known for Mexican fusion, burgers, & cheesesteaks. But, we weren't there for any of that. We were there for the all-day breakfast. Their Buttermilk pancakes were a ray of sunshine. The steak & eggs, they weren't your usual breakfast cut, featuring a SOLID piece of meat. And then there was the French Toast Chimichanga. 

I don’t even know where to begin!

It's a crisp, cinnamon covered tortilla, with eggs, cheese, sausage, and chunks of French Toast inside. This was the most creative breakfast combo I've had, and I LOVED it!

B-Unos is family owned with locations in Mt Holly, Florence, and Burlington City.

Settled in 1677, this Burlington County seat started out as Northampton, then Bridgetown, before being renamed Mount Holly in 1931. Which is kinda curious, since last time I checked, there were no mountains in Burlington County?! It turns out the "mount" in Mount Holly is just this small 180ft hill with some holly trees on it.

There's lots to see & do in town, including the original Burlington County Court house, the oldest prison in America, the oldest volunteer firefighting company in America, the Mill Race Village shops, and historic walking tours on the weekends.

Burlington County is the largest in Jersey. They like to claim it's the only county that stretches across the entire state from the Delaware to the Atlantic. 

Except it's NOT! I'm pretty sure Cape May county ALSO spans from the Delaware to the Atlantic!

Next time you're in Mt Holly, be sure to stop by B-Unos or one of the other small businesses in town!

NJ Town #113: East Newark
 
We visited @thetopsdiner Tops may have "diner" in the name, but this place is WAY more than a diner! It has that vintage diner style, but it's got a lounge-like vibe, serving well-crafted cocktails, with a full barista bar, & their own in-house bakery. Forget the traditional diner jukebox, there's even a DJ spinning tunes!!! 
 
They have a diverse menu that goes way beyond diner standards like disco fries. Grilled octopus in a diner?! This was phenomenal! Beet salad with Goat Cheese, and one of the finest French Dip sandwiches I've had. The bread was perfectly crisp, and the steak so tender. Be sure to check out their Latin grill platter too. Their Ube latte was irresistible!
 
We liked Tops so much, that we've come back multiple times!
 
Established in 1895 when the folks of the 1st Ward neighborhood of Kearny felt they were getting no respect from city hall. They voted to become their own town.
 
The lawyer who organized the vote, Edward Kenny, gets on a horse, races down to Trenton and convinces the state legislature to let them become their own town. After getting the approval, he rides back north through the night, where folks woke up in their new town, greeting him from their balconies, throwing their hats in the air and cheering the creation of East Newark while a band paraded through the streets.

It's a fantastic legend of a story, but the truth is, they just had a boring vote. Kenny did go on to become the first mayor. I guess if you were the "father" of a new town in Jersey, you'd probably boast and embellish a little too, right?
 
Next time you're in East Newark, visiting Tops Diner is a must!

New Jersey Town #112: Lakehurst

We stopped for breakfast at the Lakehurst Diner.  This place is a like a trip back  in time to when service was prompt, prices were low, and the coffee keeps flowing until you surrender!

We enjoyed a pair of Florentine omelets and the French Toast with whipped cream.

Time for town trivia…

Lakehurst is known for the Lakehurst Naval Air Station… which is actually NOT in Lakehurst, but in neighboring Manchester!

Lakehurst is one of 21 “donut hole” towns in Jersey.  It’s completely surrounded by neighboring Manchester which has a history of being a little bit hard on the folks of Lakehurst.

Years ago, Lakehurst used to be part of Manchester. (It was called “Manchester Village”!)

That was until the handful of folks who lived in the pine barrens of Manchester didn’t want to pay higher taxes for things like sidewalks, sewers, running water, and other services that were wanted by the 900-something folks living in Lakehurst at the time.

The folks of Manchester said, “You do you!” And with that Lakehurst broke away from Manchester in 1921 to become its own town so it could raise taxes for those services.

Well, little did those pineys know, Manchester would explode in population in the 1970s as retirees and folks from north Jersey moved down in droves. There’s now more than 45 thousand folks in Manchester compared to only 2600 in Lakehurst.  And I bet they ALL still hate paying these crazy Jersey taxes!

Next time you’re in Lakehurst, be sure to stop by the Lakehurst Diner, or one of the other small businesses in town!

New Jersey Town #111: East Rutherford

Trivia before food this time!

Established in 1894, East Rutherford is Jersey’s sports mecca.  Home of the Meadowlands Sports Complex, built out in the swamps of Jersey. 

Oh come on! This isn’t trivia… EVERYONE knows it’s Jersey’s promised land (of parking lots 🤣), hosting the Giants, the Jets, Horse Track Racing, and the largest concert venue in the state, MetLife Stadium.

Okay, with so many restaurants, both in town, and at the LARGEST mall in Jersey, the American Dream, some might say it’s a shame we brought in our own food.

BUT… we couldn’t say no to tailgating at a Bruce show with a Wawa picnic! (@wawa ) Hoagies, Iced Tea, Pretzels, great friends, and great music... it doesn’t get MORE Jersey than that! 

Sure, we ended up in the nose bleeds, but these were last minute tickets we scored before the show.

After nearly THREE hours of rocking, The Boss closed us out with Jersey Girl.  Life is good!

And with that, we crossed East Rutherford off the list!

Next time you’re in East Rutherford… we hope YOU’RE lucky enough to catch Bruce live too!

New Jersey Town #110: Brielle

We stopped by outdoor deck of Waypoint 622 (@waypoint622), located on the Manasquan River waterfront.

We kicked off with some celebratory cocktails as a few hours earlier, we became official "empty nesters", after dropping the youngest off at college.

It was a long day, and we were starved. So starved that we actually forgot to "bless our food" with the Ghost of Steve Jobs! 

We got a pic of the crab cakes but the pair of Lobster Rolls we didn't stand a chance. We were half way through by time we realized and took our picture with the clipboard.

Waypoint 622 used to be known as "Union Landing", and the entire marina area at the end of Union Lane as "The Union", where it was a salt works during the Revolutionary War. The British attacked it during the war but it was quickly rebuilt, as salt was vital for preserving food before refrigeration existed.

In the 1880s, a group of developers, inspired by the fortress shaped town of Brielle in The Netherlands, created the "Brielle Land Association" to develop a community between The Union and the Glimmer Glass tidal pool. In 1919, the newly developed area broke off from Wall Township, and Brielle, NJ was born.

Brielle is connected to neighboring Manasquan by the historic Glimmer Glass Bridge, built in 1888. It is a "bascule" counterweighted drawbridge, the only one of its type in the US. 

Next time you're in Brielle, be sure to stop by Waypoint 622 or one of the other great restaurants in town!

New Jersey Town #109: Irvington

We stopped by Antojitos Bar & Restaurant (@antojitosrestaurant), located on the main strip of Springfield Avenue.

We got there early when it wasn't busy, but as the night goes on, it turns into more of a club.

The music selection was great, banging with current Latin and Reggaeton hits. 

We kicked off with some classy cocktails including their Malibu Bay Breeze and a Super-sized margarita that's essentially 2 drinks in 1 with a full Corona mixed in!

We fed our ceviche addiction with the Ceviche de Camarones. 

For the mains, El Bistek Encebollado y El Churrasco Ecuatoriano trajeron los sabores de Ecuador a Jersey. ¡Tenemos suerte de tener tantas cocinas disponibles en nuestro estado!

Okay… maybe there were too many super-sized margaritas! 🤪

Established in 1874, Irvington's got talent!

Comedian Jerry Lewis, famous for hosting the annual Labor Day telethons for the Muscular Dystrophy Association graduated from Irvington High School.

Rapper and actress Queen Latifah, grew up in neighboring Newark, but graduated high school here where her mother worked as an art teacher.

While he went to high school in neighboring Maplewood, rapper Pras, better known as a member of Jersey's own Fugees, grew up in Irvington too. 

This industrial park on the border with Maplewood used to be Olympic Park, one of Jersey's largest amusement parks, from 1887 to 1965.

Part of the park still lives on today as the Prince Charming Carrousel at the Magic Kingdom in Disney World, behind the Cinderella Castle!

New Jersey Town #108: Rumson

We stopped by The French Market (@thefrenchmarketrumson) for breakfast on a late summer morning. The inside is beautifully decorated with a chic atmosphere that is warm & inviting.

We got a pair of savory crepes, one with scrambled eggs, Parisian ham & swiss, and the other with scrambled eggs, spinach, and goat cheese.  We also shared an order of their Classic French Toast made with Brioche bread that was absolutely delightful, and accompanied with fresh seasonal berries.

The café has a great selection of sandwiches, pastries and cheeses which we didn’t get to try, but they all looked great.

Time for town trivia…

Settled in 1665, the town is a peninsula surrounded by the Navesink and Shrewsbury Rivers.  The natural landscape has made Rumson a desirable place to live, and some of Jersey’s most famous residents have called it home over the years. 

But don’t expect to go celebrity hunting with a “Map of the stars” like in Hollywood. Folks take their privacy seriously around here as some of the grandest estates in town are hidden behind Rumson’s most popular form of vegetation, the hedgerow.

One place you SHOULD visit is Victory Park, located at the base of the Oceanic Bridge. The area around the park is the business district of town and features several shops and restaurants. 
And for the past few years, every May, they have an open swim on the Navesink River… which yours truly participated in 2014!

Next time you’re in Rumson, if you’re craving something savory, be sure to stop by the French Market or one of the other small businesses in town!

New Jersey Town #107: Lacey Township

We stopped by The Waterfront for dockside cocktails on the Forked River. The VooDoo Rum Bucket kicked, and who can say no to $4 Margaritas at happy hour?! (@thewaterfrontnj)

Rated one of the top "Dock & Dine" restaurants at the shore by the Asbury Park Press, we enjoyed the Lobster Bisque, General Tso's Cauliflower, and Eggplant Napolean. We weren't expecting a mostly vegetarian meal, but it worked out that way. Except, for the Hot Honey Chicken Sandwich served Oscar style with crab meat! That thing was the star of the show.

Established in 1871, Lacey has a bit of a name recognition problem!

When you enter the town, you'll see the "Welcome to Lacey Township" sign, the Lacey town hall, the giant Lacey water tower, the Lacey Library, the Lacey Community center, and even the main street through town is Lacey Rd!

BUT… no one calls this place Lacey! 🤯

Forked River is a "Census Designated place" in Lacey township. Unlike many other places in Jersey, Forked River is NOT an actual town! It's just a neighborhood.
Yet, most people refer to ALL of Lacey as Forked River!
(Except for the folks that live in the Lanoka Harbor neighborhood. 🤣) 

If you're planning to visit, you won't find it on the Garden State Parkway. You'll need to take the exit for Forked River

Confused? Welcome to Jersey! Where it's common that a town with one name is known better by another.

Next time you're in Lacey (or Forked River), be sure to stop by the Waterfront or one of the other great restaurants in town!

Time for town trivia:

Established in 1838, the town's name was derived from a Native American Lenape tribe known as the Raritan.

The south branch of the Raritan River (Jersey's largest!) forms the northeastern border of the town.

Raritan Township is a "donut" town, completely surrounding the "donut hole" town of Flemington, NJ within its borders.

New Jersey Town #104: Avon-By-The-Sea

We stopped by The Columns, a Jersey Shore classic. (@columnsnj)

We had the fried calamari, a surf n turf roll with sirloin and lobster, the Seafood Mediterranean, and a summer night at the shore wouldn't be complete without a Lobster roll!

Time for town trivia…

The Columns has been around since 1883. Around the same time, a much larger hotel was built up the street called the "Avon Inn". The Avon Inn burned down in 1978, but…

Established in 1900, the town Avon-By-The-Sea was named after this place!

IF you believe one of the two stories up for debate on the town's official website.

You can believe that Vikings sailed all the way from Greenland in search of America, and somehow randomly landed on the Jersey Shore just north of the Shark River
 and named it "A-von-ee" after their fearless leader.

OR…

You can believe that a rich tobacco magnate from Philadelphia bought the land, laid out the streets of the town, built the hotel, and named the Avon Inn after Avon, England.

As much as I'd love to believe that fist pumping Vikings landed on the Jersey Shore, I'm going with the Philly Tobacco magnate and Avon, England.

No matter what you believe, next time you're down in Avon By The Sea, be sure to hit up the Columns for a fun night out!

Bonus trivia:

Avon-By-The-Sea is also one of only 5 towns in Jersey with hyphens its official name!

As of 2000, 36.5% of residents were of Irish ancestry, the 3rd highest percentage of ANY municipality in the USA!

New Jersey Town #103: Englishtown

Ah yes, Englishtown, the “downtown” of my hometown of Manalapan. 

We stopped by Cholula’s Mexican Restaurant.
We enjoyed the Ceviche Mixto, my favorite, el Pollo Mole Poblano, and a burrito bowl.

Time for Town Trivia:

Settled in 1688, Englishtown is the downtown at the HEART of my hometown of Manalapan.
It’s entirely surrounded by Manalapan, making it one of 21 “donut hole” towns in Jersey. This pattern exists all over Jersey. Typically, an old village breaks off to be its own independent town from a larger, more rural, township around it.

Growing up, everyone used to say George Washington slept here. There’s no official record of that, but Washington definitely was not happy while visiting. He ordered a court marshal here for one of his generals who retreated prematurely from the Battle of Monmouth in Manalapan. Washington had to step in and lead the battle that forced the British to retreat from New Jersey.

They used to make silverware here at the old Washington Forge factory. These days, a new economy is budding in town with at least 4 cannabis dispensaries recently opening.

Next time you have the munchies, be sure to stop by Cholula’s or one of the other small businesses in town!

New Jersey Town #100: Brick

We ate at our friends annual Pig Roast! And ate some more. And got way too much sun on a summer afternoon.

When visiting towns in Jersey, eating at a friend or family member’s home counts towards our #eatjerseychallenge.  We may get some flak for that, but there are some towns with NO restaurants, so eating at someone’s home or bringing food from elsewhere to have a picnic is how we’ll handle that.  Brick, NJ has PLENTY of great restaurants, so that was not the case here.  But since we ate a meal, we counted it!

Time for town trivia:

Established in 1850, the town was named for Joseph W. Brick who owned the Bergen Iron Works in town. After he died at age 42, his kids named the neighborhood around the factory as “Bricksburg” to honor their father.

But then, in 1892, “Bricksburg” broke away from Brick Township to form its own town, which became Lakewood, NJ!  That means the person who Brick is named after actually lived in what is now Lakewood, NJ.

In 1963, the town considered changing its name to Laurelton, but the people voted against it.  Despite the fact that the Brick family split their section of town off to form Lakewood, the people of Brick Township didn’t care to change the town’s name!

If you’re visiting Brick, we can’t guarantee you an invite to our friends’ pig roast. 😎 But we recommend you try one of the many great restaurants in town.

New Jersey Town #94: Watchung

We stopped by the POURtuga Coffee House (@pourtugacoffeehouse ).

Having visited Portugal 2022, it was like being back in Lisbon. The toasted ham & cheese Portuguese croissants were great and the Pastel de Nata super fresh! The coffee shop has a great vibe and is a great place to hang out and have a relaxing breakfast. 

This was our first stop on the #eatjerseychallenge that was shared as a recommendation. Thanks to dubchamber for letting us know about this great spot! We love recommendations for towns we visit, so if you have them, put them in the comments.

Time for town trivia:

Established in 1926, the town had a bit of a name crisis when it was born.

During the Revolutionary War, George Washington used to pass through the area frequently to spy on British troops from the top of the first ridge of Watchung Mountains. It enabled him to observe troop movements 20 miles away in Perth Amboy. As a result, the area was known as “Washingtonville” and was part of what is now Green Brook Township. 

When the town was created in 1926, the US Post Office said NO to “Washingtonville” for the new town’s name given that there were already too many towns named “Washington” in the state! (There were seven at the time… and we’ll talk about them in future posts.) The town then settled on Watchung, named for the mountain ridge Washington used to spy on the Brits.

The name “Watchung” comes from the Lenni Lenape Native American word “watschu”, meaning “high hills”.

New Jersey Town #93: Carteret

We visited Hornitos Bakery & Restaurant (@hornitoscarteretnj ).

On the menu at this stop:

Ceviche Colombiano,

and of course… a sweet ending with Tres Leches cake.

Time for town trivia:

Established in 1906 as “Roosevelt” borough, the town’s name was changed to Carteret in 1922 after Sir George Carteret. 

Sorry Teddy Roosevelt… At least they named the town’s main street after you.

It turns out Carteret is the guy that gave New Jersey its name!

Back when the King of England was carving up his colonies in the Americas, he granted the land we know as New Jersey to Carteret as a gift for saving the king from death during a battle in English Civil War.

Carteret was from the island of Jersey (aka “OLD Jersey”) back in England, and decided to call his war prize “NEW Jersey”. Of course, he didn’t ask the native Lenni Lenape for THEIR opinion. Carteret never set foot in the Americas. But his choice of name choice stuck around, and that’s how our state got its name!

If you’re ever hungry while passing Exit 12 on the Turnpike, be sure to stop by Hornitos in Carteret!

Settled in 1684, South Amboy has a LOT of history.

In 1950, one event blew the rest out of the water… literally.

A giant explosion occurred when dock workers were transferring ammunition from freight train cars onto barges in the harbor. 

31 people were killed, and almost ALL of South Amboy’s 2700 homes and buildings suffered damage and had their windows blown out!

New Jersey Town #91: Allenhurst

It may be cold in Jersey when this post hits, but it was a perfect beach day down the shore when we hit up Mister C’s in Allenhurst! @mistercs_ 

After hanging on the hot beach all day, Mister C’s was a great place to go for some ice cold rum buckets, fresh lobster rolls, and live music!  The restaurant was adjacent to the Allenhurst Beach Club and had great ocean views from the outdoor deck.

Unfortunately, I’m using the past tense here because Mister C’s closed at the end of the 2024 summer season as their lease was up at the beach club.  It was just announced in the Asbury Park Press that a new restaurant will be opening up in summer 2025 after doing some major renovations on the space. With that location, I’m sure the new place will do great and we’d look forward to checking it out.

Established in 1897, Allenhurst is one the 20 smallest towns land-wise in the state. In 1895, the Coast Land Improvement Company bought a 120 acre farm in Ocean Township from Abner Allen for the purpose of building an exclusive resort community. Having a train station right there led to success, and two years later, the new neighborhood broke away from Ocean Township to become its own town named after Allen.

Tiny as it is, Allenhurst remains one of the wealthiest towns along the Jersey Shore today with beautiful, well maintained, turn-of-the-century homes..

New Jersey Town #90: Colts Neck

We stopped by the Colts Neck Eatery (@colts_neck_eatery ) for breakfast on our way down the shore for a beach day.  Great eats in what  is essentially a Jersey-style diner tucked away in a non-descript strip mall near the center of town. We enjoyed the Arugula Avocado and Country Fried Steak & Eggs for breakfast. 

When we started the #eatjerseychallenge, we thought it would be just a “checkbox exercise” to eat in each town.  We didn’t know we’d be posting about town trivia or trying to actually explore the towns we visited.  So these first few towns we’re posting about don’t have much information, or as many food pics. 

Established in 1847 as “Atlantic Township”, the name was changed to “Colts Neck” in 1962. Perhaps because it is known for its large number of equestrian farms?  The town is one of the wealthiest in the state, and is currently home to one of Jersey’s most famous residents, a guy named Bruce.

If you’re visiting Colts Neck, be sure to check out the Colts Neck Eatery, or one of the other great establishments in town like Delicious Orchards, Rook Coffee, The Source Brewery, or Colts Neck Stillhouse.

New Jersey Town #89: Atlantic Highlands

During summer, after a long day of work in New York City, it’s time to escape the rat race and hop the ferry from midtown down the shore to Atlantic Highlands.

Step off the boat, and climb the stairs to On The Deck. (@otdrestaurant ) 
You’ll be listening to live music and sipping rum buckets in no time!
Soak in the views of Sandy Hook and Raritan Bay while you enjoy dinner, like we did.

We had the Prince Edward Island mussels in white sauce and Spicy Shrimp voodoo-style with cornbread.
And, did I mention the rum buckets?!

Time for town trivia:

Established in 1887, Atlantic Highlands is named for the “high lands” in town, which, at 266ft above sea level form the highest point on the east coast between Maine and Florida.

Head up the hill from downtown and check out the Mt Mitchell Scenic overlook with stunning views of the coast and NYC Skyline. It’s also the home of Monmouth County’s 9/11 Memorial.

Next time your in Atlantic Highlands, be sure to check out On The Deck, or one of the other great restaurants in town!

Inspired by friends in Massachusetts, we started the #eatjerseychallenge in July 2023.

There are officially 564 towns in New Jersey. We're going to eat a meal in each town and learn more about our state, one town at a time.

Here are the "rules":
1. Eat a meal in every town in NJ
2. Must eat all meals with your challenge partner (unless doing the challenge solo!)
3. A "meal" can be breakfast, lunch, dinner, or dessert (the best of them all!)
4. Meals at a friend/family's home count.
5. If a town has no restaurants, make a friend to invite you to dinner or bring food from elsewhere and have a picnic.
6. You CAN retroactively count towns before you start the challenge if you know a specific date & location or restaurant name.
